title: Severe Leg Pain After Fracture - Compartment Syndrome
chief_complaint: 25-year-old male involved in motorcycle accident 6 hours ago. Closed tibial shaft fracture splinted. Now severe progressive leg pain out of proportion to injury, not relieved by opioids. Tense swollen leg.

physical_exam.leg: Tense, swollen anterior compartment, pain with passive stretch of toes, decreased sensation first web space (deep peroneal nerve), dorsalis pedis pulse weakly palpable, capillary refill 4 seconds

hidden_data.compartment_pressure: Anterior compartment: 65 mmHg (critical, normal <10-15, delta pressure <30 = fasciotomy)
hidden_data.xray_leg: Comminuted midshaft tibia and fibula fracture

correct_diagnosis: Acute Compartment Syndrome (Anterior Leg) with Tibia-Fibula Fracture
key_learning_points: 6 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia (LATE signs)
Pain out of proportion + pain with passive stretch = EARLY signs
Time is muscle - irreversible damage in 4-6 hours

patient_responses.pain: The pain is unbearable, morphine didn't help
patient_responses.numbness: I can't feel between my big toe and second toe
patient_responses.accident: Motorcycle hit a car, my leg took the impact

feedback.correct_message: Excellent! Compartment syndrome is a true orthopedic emergency.
feedback.incorrect_message: This is compartment syndrome. The pain out of proportion + compartment pressures confirm it.
feedback.attending_note: Compartment syndrome: Emergency four-compartment fasciotomy. Do NOT wait for pulselessness (late sign). Leave wounds open, return to OR in 48-72h for closure or skin graft. Irreversible muscle and nerve damage occurs in 4-6 hours.
